Appendix A P-nodes: Variable Embedding - Missing Cases from Subsection 6.5.2
                        Appendix A P-nodes: Variable Embedding - Missing Cases from Subsection 6.5.2
In this section, we give necessary and sufficient conditions under which a P-node admits one of the relevant embedding types , , , , , , , , and . The conditions for  are discussed in Subsection 6.5.2. The mentioned embedding types are enclosed by a solid polygon in Fig. 12.Conditions for Type, Type, and Type.
Lemma 13
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if all of its children admit Type .
Proof
For the necessity, let \(\gamma \) be any \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type . Let \(\mu ^*\) be any child of \(\mu \) and let \(\gamma ^*\) be the \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu ^*})\) obtained by restricting \(\gamma \) to \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu ^*})\). Since \(\gamma \) is of Type , there exist in \(\gamma ^*\): (i) a portion of the spine incident to \(s_\mu \) and between \(s_\mu \) and \(t_\mu \) that is visible from the left page, and (ii) a portion of the spine incident to \(t_\mu \) and between \(s_\mu \) and \(t_\mu \) that is visible from the right page. Thus, \(\gamma ^*\) has Type , i.e., \(\mu ^*\) admits Type . For the sufficiency, we order the children of \(\mu \) arbitrarily; see Fig. 17a for an example. Clearly, the resulting \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) is of Type .
\(\square \)
Fig. 17Constructions for P-nodes of Type , , and Full size imageThe next lemma is proved similarly to Lemma 13; see Fig. 17b for an example.
Lemma 14
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if all of its children admit Type .
Lemma 15
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if it is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into two sets as follows: The first set consists either of a Q-node child \(\omega \) or of a child \(\xi \) that admits Type , or both; the second set consists of any number, possibly zero, of children admitting Type .
Proof
The necessity is easy to see. In fact, for any child \(\mu _i\) of \(\mu \), the restriction of a \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  to \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu _i})\) is either of Type  or of Type . Also, a \(2\)UBE of Type  for \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) is incompatible with more than one \(2\)UBE of Type  for its children, unless one of them is a single edge. If no child of \(\mu \) is such that the \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) is of Type , then the \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) would have Type .
About the sufficiency: we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have: the Q-node \(\omega \), if any, with Type , followed by \(\xi \), if any, followed by the remaining children of \(\mu \) in any order; see Fig. 17c. \(\square \)
Conditions for Type . Let \(\mu \) be a P-node, we define two cases:NBR.1 All children of \(\mu \) admit either Type  or Type , and there exist at least a child that admits Type  and a child that admits Type .NBR.2 There exists a child of \(\mu \) that admits Type  and all other children admit either Type  or Type .
Lemma 16
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if Case NBR.1 or NBR.2 holds.
Proof
It is easy to see that these conditions are necessary. In fact, for any child \(\mu _i\) of \(\mu \), the restriction of a \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  to \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu _i})\) is clearly of Type \(\langle X, B, R\rangle \), with \(X \in \{R,L,N\}\), since a \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of type \(\langle S, B, T\rangle \) implies that the embeddings of the \(2\)UBEs of the pertinent graphs of all children of \(\mu \) must be of type \(\langle S', B, T'\rangle \), with \(S,S',T,T' \in \{R,L,N\}\).Fig. 18Constructions for P-nodes of Type Full size image
For the sufficiency, both in Case NBR.1 and in Case NBR.2 we order the children from left to right in such a way that children with \(2\)UBEs of Type  precede all children with \(2\)UBEs of Type ; see Figs.18a,18b. In Case NBR.2, the child with \(2\)UBE of Type  is placed between the last child with \(2\)UBE of Type , if any, and the first child with \(2\)UBE of Type , if any; see Fig. 18b. \(\square \)
Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node, we define two cases:RBN.1 All children of \(\mu \) admit either Type  or Type , and there exist at least a child that admits Type  and a child that admits Type .RBN.2 There exists a child of \(\mu \) that admits Type  and all other children admit either Type  or Type .
Lemma 17
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if Case RBN.1 or RBN.2 holds.
Proof
Cases RBN.1 and RBN.2 are symmetric to Cases NBR.1 and NBR.2, i.e., they can be obtained from Cases NBR.1 and NBR.2 after a vertical flip of the realizable embedding types of the children of \(\mu \). Thus, the necessity and sufficiency proofs are symmetric to those presented in Lemma 16. \(\square \)
Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node. We define six cases:NBN.1 There exists a child of \(\mu \) that admits Type  and the other children admit either Type  or Type .NBN.2 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into three sets as follows. The first set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type . The second set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type . The third set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .NBN.3 This case is obtained from Case NBN.2 by considering the types admitted by its children after a vertical flip.NBN.4–NBN.6 These cases are obtained from Cases NBN.1–NBN.3, respectively, by considering the horizontally-mirrored embedding types for the children of \(\mu \).
Lemma 18
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if at least one of Cases NBN.1–NBN.6 holds.
Proof
Since Type  is self-symmetric, we have that, for each case NBN.1–NBN.3, we can obtain a symmetric one (i.e., cases NBN.4–NBN.6) by reversing the left-to-right sequence of the children in the construction and by taking, for each child, the horizontally-mirrored embedding type. Thus, for each pair of symmetric cases, we only describe one of them.
For the sufficiency, we consider the three cases separately. In Case NBN.1 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the children that admit Type  (if any), in any order, followed by the unique child that admits Type , followed by the children that admit Type  (if any), in any order; see Fig. 19a. In Case NBN.2 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the possible children that admit Type  (if any), in any order, followed by the possible child that admits only Type  (if any), followed by the possible children that admit Type  (if any), in any order, followed by the possible child that admits only Type  (if any), followed by the possible children that admit Type  (if any), in any order (see Fig. 19b). In Case NBN.3 we proceed as in Case NBN.2 by considering the types after a vertical flip. Clearly, these orderings allow for a \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type .Fig. 19Constructions for P-nodes of Type Full size image
The necessity can be argued by considering that in any 2UBE \(\gamma \) of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  the following hold: at most one of the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle N ,\cdot , \cdot \rangle \); at most one of the restriction of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle \cdot ,\cdot , N \rangle \); and all the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) are of Type \(\langle \cdot ,B, \cdot \rangle \). \(\square \)
Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node. We define two cases:RRN.1 The children of \(\mu \) can be partitioned into two sets: The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type .RRN.2 The children of \(\mu \) can be partitioned into three sets: The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The second part consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type . The third set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .
Lemma 19
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if Case RRN.1 or Case RRN.2 holds.
Proof
Observe that Cases RRN.1 and RRN.2 are symmetric to Cases NRR.1 and NRR.2 (see Lemma 12), i.e., they can be obtained from Cases NRR.1 and NRR.2 after a vertical flip of the types of the children. The necessity and sufficiency proofs are symmetric to those presented in Lemma 12. \(\square \)
Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node, we define six cases:NRN.1 There exists a child that admits Type  and the other non-Q-node children admit Type .NRN.2 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into three sets as follows. The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type . The third set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .NRN.3 This case is obtained from Case NRN.2 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.NRN.4 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into two sets as follows. The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .NRN.5 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into three sets as follows. The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type . The third set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .NRN.6 This case is obtained from Case NRN.5 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.
Lemma 20
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if one of Cases NRN.1–NRN.6 holds.
Fig. 20Constructions for P-nodes of Type Full size image
Proof
For the sufficiency, we consider the six cases separately. In Case NRN.1 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (if any), with Type , followed by the unique Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order; see Fig. 20a.
In Case NRN.2 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (if any), with Type , followed by the Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the unique Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order; see 20b. In Case NRN.3 we proceed as in Case NRN.2 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NRN.4 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (if any), with Type , followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the unique Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order; see Fig. 20c. In Case NRN.5 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (if any), with Type , followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the unique Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the unique Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order; see Fig. 20d. In Case NRN.6 we proceed as in Case NRN.5 by considering the types after a vertical flip.
The necessity can be argued by considering that in any 2UBE \(\gamma \) of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  we have the following: at most one of the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle N ,\cdot , \cdot \rangle \); at most one of the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle \cdot ,\cdot , N \rangle \); and that all the restrictions of \(\gamma \) on the children of \(\mu \) must be of Type \(\langle \cdot ,R, \cdot \rangle \) or of Type \(\langle \cdot ,B, \cdot \rangle \). However, not all the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) may be of Type \(\langle \cdot ,B, \cdot \rangle \) as otherwise the resulting embeddings would be of Type \(\langle \cdot ,B, \cdot \rangle \). \(\square \)
Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node. In order to describe when \(\mu \) is of Type , we need to define twenty-six cases. We assume that Case NNN.i applies only if Cases NNN.k, with \(1 \le k \le i-1\), do not apply.NNN.1 There exist two children, one of which is a Q-node and the other admits Type .NNN.2 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into three sets as follows. The first set consists of a child that admits Type ; the second set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The third set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type .NNN.3 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into five sets as follows. The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of any number of children that admit Type . The third set consists of a child that admits Type . The fourth set consists of any number of children that admit Type . The fifth set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both.NNN.4 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into four sets as follows. The first set consists of one child that admits Type . The second set consists of any number of children that admit Type  or Type . The third set consists of at most one child that admits only Type . The fourth set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both.NNN.5 This case is obtained from Case NNN.4 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.NNN.6 There exists a child that admits Type , any number of children that admit Type , at most one child that admits only Type , and at least one child that is either a Q-node or admits Type .NNN.7 This case is obtained from Case NNN.6 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.NNN.8 There exists a child that admits Type , at most one child that admits only Type , any number of children that admit Type , Type , or Type . Furthermore, there exist either a non-Q-node child that admits Type  or a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both; if only one of such children exists then \(\mu \) has also a Q-node child.NNN.9 This case is obtained from Case NNN.8 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.NNN.10 There exists a child that admits Type , and any number of children that admit Type  or Type . Furthermore, there exist either a non-Q-node child that admits Type  or a child that admits Type , or both; if only one of such children exists then \(\mu \) has also a Q-node child.NNN.11 This case is obtained from Case NNN.10 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.NNN.12 There exists a non-Q-node child that admits Type , any number of children that admit Type  or Type , and either a Q-node child or a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both.NNN.13 This case is obtained from Case NNN.12 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.NNN.14–NNN.26 These cases are obtained from Cases NNN.1–NNN.13, respectively, by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a horizontal mirroring.
Lemma 21
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if one of Cases NNN.1–NNN.26 holds.
Fig. 21Constructions for P-nodes of Type . Subfigures (c) and (d) show the constructions for Case NNN.3 when there exists no Type  non-Q-node child and when there exists such a child, respectivelyFull size imageFig. 22Constructions for P-nodes of Type . Proof
Since Type  is self-symmetric, we only need to consider the Cases NNN.1–NNN.13; the Cases NNN.14–NNN.26 being analogous. In Case NNN.1 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child, with Type , followed by the Type  child; see Fig. 21a. In Case NNN.2 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by Q-node child (if any), with Type ; see Fig 21b. In Case NNN.3 we proceed as follows. If there exists no Type  non-Q-node child, we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case), with Type , followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case). See Fig. 21c. Otherwise (there exists one Type  non-Q-node child), we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  non-Q-node child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the Type  Q-node child, if any. See Fig.21d. In Case NNN.4 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the Type  Q-node child (if any). See Fig. 22a. In Case NNN.5 we proceed as in Case NNN.4 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NNN.6 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  child, followed by any number of Type  children, followed by the child that admits only Type  (if any), followed by the Q-node child (if any), with Type ; see Fig. 22b. In Case NNN.7 we proceed as in Case NNN.6 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NNN.8 we proceed as follows. If there exists no Type  non-Q-node child, we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case), with Type , followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (if any) followed by the Type  children, if any, in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case). See Fig. 22c . Otherwise (there exists one Type  non-Q-node child), we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  non-Q-node child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the Q-node child (if any), with Type ; see Fig. 22d. In Case NNN.9 we proceed as in Case NNN.8 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NNN.10 we proceed as follows. If there exists no Type  non-Q-node child, we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case), with Type , followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (which exists by the conditions of the case). See Fig. 23a. Otherwise (there exists one Type  non-Q-node child), we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  non-Q-node child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (if any), followed by the Q-node child (if any), with Type . See Fig. 23b. In Case NNN.11 we proceed as in Case NNN.10 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NNN.12 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the non-Q-node Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the Q-node child (if any), with Type ; see Fig. 23c. In Case NNN.13 we proceed as in Case NNN.12 by considering the types after a vertical flip.Fig. 23Constructions for P-nodes of Type . The necessity can be argued by considering that in any 2UBE \(\gamma \) of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  we have the following: at most one of the restriction of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle N ,\cdot , \cdot \rangle \) and at most one of the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle \cdot ,\cdot , N \rangle \) (notice that these two children may coincide in a single child of Type \(\langle N ,\cdot , N \rangle \)); at most one of the restriction of \(\gamma \) to the non-Q-node children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle L, L, L \rangle \) and at most one of the restriction of \(\gamma \) to the non-Q-node children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle R, R, R \rangle \). \(\square \)

Appendix A P-nodes: Variable Embedding - Missing Cases from Subsection 6.5.2
In this section, we give necessary and sufficient conditions under which a P-node admits one of the relevant embedding types , , , , , , , , and . The conditions for  are discussed in Subsection 6.5.2. The mentioned embedding types are enclosed by a solid polygon in Fig. 12.Conditions for Type, Type, and Type.
Lemma 13
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if all of its children admit Type .
Proof
For the necessity, let \(\gamma \) be any \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type . Let \(\mu ^*\) be any child of \(\mu \) and let \(\gamma ^*\) be the \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu ^*})\) obtained by restricting \(\gamma \) to \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu ^*})\). Since \(\gamma \) is of Type , there exist in \(\gamma ^*\): (i) a portion of the spine incident to \(s_\mu \) and between \(s_\mu \) and \(t_\mu \) that is visible from the left page, and (ii) a portion of the spine incident to \(t_\mu \) and between \(s_\mu \) and \(t_\mu \) that is visible from the right page. Thus, \(\gamma ^*\) has Type , i.e., \(\mu ^*\) admits Type . For the sufficiency, we order the children of \(\mu \) arbitrarily; see Fig. 17a for an example. Clearly, the resulting \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) is of Type .
\(\square \)
Fig. 17Constructions for P-nodes of Type , , and Full size imageThe next lemma is proved similarly to Lemma 13; see Fig. 17b for an example.
Lemma 14
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if all of its children admit Type .
Lemma 15
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if it is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into two sets as follows: The first set consists either of a Q-node child \(\omega \) or of a child \(\xi \) that admits Type , or both; the second set consists of any number, possibly zero, of children admitting Type .
Proof
The necessity is easy to see. In fact, for any child \(\mu _i\) of \(\mu \), the restriction of a \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  to \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu _i})\) is either of Type  or of Type . Also, a \(2\)UBE of Type  for \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) is incompatible with more than one \(2\)UBE of Type  for its children, unless one of them is a single edge. If no child of \(\mu \) is such that the \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) is of Type , then the \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) would have Type .
About the sufficiency: we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have: the Q-node \(\omega \), if any, with Type , followed by \(\xi \), if any, followed by the remaining children of \(\mu \) in any order; see Fig. 17c. \(\square \)
Conditions for Type . Let \(\mu \) be a P-node, we define two cases:NBR.1 All children of \(\mu \) admit either Type  or Type , and there exist at least a child that admits Type  and a child that admits Type .NBR.2 There exists a child of \(\mu \) that admits Type  and all other children admit either Type  or Type .
Lemma 16
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if Case NBR.1 or NBR.2 holds.
Proof
It is easy to see that these conditions are necessary. In fact, for any child \(\mu _i\) of \(\mu \), the restriction of a \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  to \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu _i})\) is clearly of Type \(\langle X, B, R\rangle \), with \(X \in \{R,L,N\}\), since a \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of type \(\langle S, B, T\rangle \) implies that the embeddings of the \(2\)UBEs of the pertinent graphs of all children of \(\mu \) must be of type \(\langle S', B, T'\rangle \), with \(S,S',T,T' \in \{R,L,N\}\).Fig. 18Constructions for P-nodes of Type Full size image
For the sufficiency, both in Case NBR.1 and in Case NBR.2 we order the children from left to right in such a way that children with \(2\)UBEs of Type  precede all children with \(2\)UBEs of Type ; see Figs.18a,18b. In Case NBR.2, the child with \(2\)UBE of Type  is placed between the last child with \(2\)UBE of Type , if any, and the first child with \(2\)UBE of Type , if any; see Fig. 18b. \(\square \)
Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node, we define two cases:RBN.1 All children of \(\mu \) admit either Type  or Type , and there exist at least a child that admits Type  and a child that admits Type .RBN.2 There exists a child of \(\mu \) that admits Type  and all other children admit either Type  or Type .
Lemma 17
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if Case RBN.1 or RBN.2 holds.
Proof
Cases RBN.1 and RBN.2 are symmetric to Cases NBR.1 and NBR.2, i.e., they can be obtained from Cases NBR.1 and NBR.2 after a vertical flip of the realizable embedding types of the children of \(\mu \). Thus, the necessity and sufficiency proofs are symmetric to those presented in Lemma 16. \(\square \)
Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node. We define six cases:NBN.1 There exists a child of \(\mu \) that admits Type  and the other children admit either Type  or Type .NBN.2 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into three sets as follows. The first set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type . The second set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type . The third set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .NBN.3 This case is obtained from Case NBN.2 by considering the types admitted by its children after a vertical flip.NBN.4–NBN.6 These cases are obtained from Cases NBN.1–NBN.3, respectively, by considering the horizontally-mirrored embedding types for the children of \(\mu \).
Lemma 18
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if at least one of Cases NBN.1–NBN.6 holds.
Proof
Since Type  is self-symmetric, we have that, for each case NBN.1–NBN.3, we can obtain a symmetric one (i.e., cases NBN.4–NBN.6) by reversing the left-to-right sequence of the children in the construction and by taking, for each child, the horizontally-mirrored embedding type. Thus, for each pair of symmetric cases, we only describe one of them.
For the sufficiency, we consider the three cases separately. In Case NBN.1 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the children that admit Type  (if any), in any order, followed by the unique child that admits Type , followed by the children that admit Type  (if any), in any order; see Fig. 19a. In Case NBN.2 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the possible children that admit Type  (if any), in any order, followed by the possible child that admits only Type  (if any), followed by the possible children that admit Type  (if any), in any order, followed by the possible child that admits only Type  (if any), followed by the possible children that admit Type  (if any), in any order (see Fig. 19b). In Case NBN.3 we proceed as in Case NBN.2 by considering the types after a vertical flip. Clearly, these orderings allow for a \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type .Fig. 19Constructions for P-nodes of Type Full size image
The necessity can be argued by considering that in any 2UBE \(\gamma \) of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  the following hold: at most one of the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle N ,\cdot , \cdot \rangle \); at most one of the restriction of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle \cdot ,\cdot , N \rangle \); and all the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) are of Type \(\langle \cdot ,B, \cdot \rangle \). \(\square \)
Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node. We define two cases:RRN.1 The children of \(\mu \) can be partitioned into two sets: The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type .RRN.2 The children of \(\mu \) can be partitioned into three sets: The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The second part consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type . The third set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .
Lemma 19
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if Case RRN.1 or Case RRN.2 holds.
Proof
Observe that Cases RRN.1 and RRN.2 are symmetric to Cases NRR.1 and NRR.2 (see Lemma 12), i.e., they can be obtained from Cases NRR.1 and NRR.2 after a vertical flip of the types of the children. The necessity and sufficiency proofs are symmetric to those presented in Lemma 12. \(\square \)
Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node, we define six cases:NRN.1 There exists a child that admits Type  and the other non-Q-node children admit Type .NRN.2 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into three sets as follows. The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type . The third set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .NRN.3 This case is obtained from Case NRN.2 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.NRN.4 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into two sets as follows. The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .NRN.5 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into three sets as follows. The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type . The third set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .NRN.6 This case is obtained from Case NRN.5 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.
Lemma 20
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if one of Cases NRN.1–NRN.6 holds.
Fig. 20Constructions for P-nodes of Type Full size image
Proof
For the sufficiency, we consider the six cases separately. In Case NRN.1 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (if any), with Type , followed by the unique Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order; see Fig. 20a.
In Case NRN.2 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (if any), with Type , followed by the Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the unique Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order; see 20b. In Case NRN.3 we proceed as in Case NRN.2 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NRN.4 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (if any), with Type , followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the unique Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order; see Fig. 20c. In Case NRN.5 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (if any), with Type , followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the unique Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the unique Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order; see Fig. 20d. In Case NRN.6 we proceed as in Case NRN.5 by considering the types after a vertical flip.
The necessity can be argued by considering that in any 2UBE \(\gamma \) of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  we have the following: at most one of the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle N ,\cdot , \cdot \rangle \); at most one of the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle \cdot ,\cdot , N \rangle \); and that all the restrictions of \(\gamma \) on the children of \(\mu \) must be of Type \(\langle \cdot ,R, \cdot \rangle \) or of Type \(\langle \cdot ,B, \cdot \rangle \). However, not all the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) may be of Type \(\langle \cdot ,B, \cdot \rangle \) as otherwise the resulting embeddings would be of Type \(\langle \cdot ,B, \cdot \rangle \). \(\square \)
Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node. In order to describe when \(\mu \) is of Type , we need to define twenty-six cases. We assume that Case NNN.i applies only if Cases NNN.k, with \(1 \le k \le i-1\), do not apply.NNN.1 There exist two children, one of which is a Q-node and the other admits Type .NNN.2 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into three sets as follows. The first set consists of a child that admits Type ; the second set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The third set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type .NNN.3 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into five sets as follows. The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of any number of children that admit Type . The third set consists of a child that admits Type . The fourth set consists of any number of children that admit Type . The fifth set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both.NNN.4 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into four sets as follows. The first set consists of one child that admits Type . The second set consists of any number of children that admit Type  or Type . The third set consists of at most one child that admits only Type . The fourth set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both.NNN.5 This case is obtained from Case NNN.4 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.NNN.6 There exists a child that admits Type , any number of children that admit Type , at most one child that admits only Type , and at least one child that is either a Q-node or admits Type .NNN.7 This case is obtained from Case NNN.6 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.NNN.8 There exists a child that admits Type , at most one child that admits only Type , any number of children that admit Type , Type , or Type . Furthermore, there exist either a non-Q-node child that admits Type  or a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both; if only one of such children exists then \(\mu \) has also a Q-node child.NNN.9 This case is obtained from Case NNN.8 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.NNN.10 There exists a child that admits Type , and any number of children that admit Type  or Type . Furthermore, there exist either a non-Q-node child that admits Type  or a child that admits Type , or both; if only one of such children exists then \(\mu \) has also a Q-node child.NNN.11 This case is obtained from Case NNN.10 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.NNN.12 There exists a non-Q-node child that admits Type , any number of children that admit Type  or Type , and either a Q-node child or a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both.NNN.13 This case is obtained from Case NNN.12 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.NNN.14–NNN.26 These cases are obtained from Cases NNN.1–NNN.13, respectively, by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a horizontal mirroring.
Lemma 21
(Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if one of Cases NNN.1–NNN.26 holds.
Fig. 21Constructions for P-nodes of Type . Proof
Since Type  is self-symmetric, we only need to consider the Cases NNN.1–NNN.13; the Cases NNN.14–NNN.26 being analogous. In Case NNN.1 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child, with Type , followed by the Type  child; see Fig. 21a. In Case NNN.2 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by Q-node child (if any), with Type ; see Fig 21b. In Case NNN.3 we proceed as follows. If there exists no Type  non-Q-node child, we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case), with Type , followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case). See Fig. 21c. Otherwise (there exists one Type  non-Q-node child), we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  non-Q-node child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the Type  Q-node child, if any. See Fig.21d. In Case NNN.4 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the Type  Q-node child (if any). See Fig. 22a. In Case NNN.5 we proceed as in Case NNN.4 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NNN.6 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  child, followed by any number of Type  children, followed by the child that admits only Type  (if any), followed by the Q-node child (if any), with Type ; see Fig. 22b. In Case NNN.7 we proceed as in Case NNN.6 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NNN.8 we proceed as follows. If there exists no Type  non-Q-node child, we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case), with Type , followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (if any) followed by the Type  children, if any, in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case). See Fig. 22c . Otherwise (there exists one Type  non-Q-node child), we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  non-Q-node child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the Q-node child (if any), with Type ; see Fig. 22d. In Case NNN.9 we proceed as in Case NNN.8 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NNN.10 we proceed as follows. If there exists no Type  non-Q-node child, we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case), with Type , followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (which exists by the conditions of the case). See Fig. 23a. Otherwise (there exists one Type  non-Q-node child), we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  non-Q-node child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (if any), followed by the Q-node child (if any), with Type . See Fig. 23b. In Case NNN.11 we proceed as in Case NNN.10 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NNN.12 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the non-Q-node Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the Q-node child (if any), with Type ; see Fig. 23c. In Case NNN.13 we proceed as in Case NNN.12 by considering the types after a vertical flip.Fig. 23Constructions for P-nodes of Type . The necessity can be argued by considering that in any 2UBE \(\gamma \) of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  we have the following: at most one of the restriction of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle N ,\cdot , \cdot \rangle \) and at most one of the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle \cdot ,\cdot , N \rangle \) (notice that these two children may coincide in a single child of Type \(\langle N ,\cdot , N \rangle \)); at most one of the restriction of \(\gamma \) to the non-Q-node children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle L, L, L \rangle \) and at most one of the restriction of \(\gamma \) to the non-Q-node children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle R, R, R \rangle \). \(\square \)

text_blocks.text: Appendix A P-nodes: Variable Embedding - Missing Cases from Subsection 6.5.2
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: In this section, we give necessary and sufficient conditions under which a P-node admits one of the relevant embedding types , , , , , , , , and . The conditions for  are discussed in Subsection 6.5.2. The mentioned embedding types are enclosed by a solid polygon in Fig. 12.
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: Conditions for Type, Type, and Type.
text_blocks.label: other

text_blocks.text: (Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if all of its children admit Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: For the necessity, let \(\gamma \) be any \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type . Let \(\mu ^*\) be any child of \(\mu \) and let \(\gamma ^*\) be the \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu ^*})\) obtained by restricting \(\gamma \) to \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu ^*})\). Since \(\gamma \) is of Type , there exist in \(\gamma ^*\): (i) a portion of the spine incident to \(s_\mu \) and between \(s_\mu \) and \(t_\mu \) that is visible from the left page, and (ii) a portion of the spine incident to \(t_\mu \) and between \(s_\mu \) and \(t_\mu \) that is visible from the right page. Thus, \(\gamma ^*\) has Type , i.e., \(\mu ^*\) admits Type . For the sufficiency, we order the children of \(\mu \) arbitrarily; see Fig. 17a for an example. Clearly, the resulting \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) is of Type .
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: The next lemma is proved similarly to Lemma 13; see Fig. 17b for an example.
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: (Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if all of its children admit Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: (Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if it is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into two sets as follows: The first set consists either of a Q-node child \(\omega \) or of a child \(\xi \) that admits Type , or both; the second set consists of any number, possibly zero, of children admitting Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: The necessity is easy to see. In fact, for any child \(\mu _i\) of \(\mu \), the restriction of a \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  to \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu _i})\) is either of Type  or of Type . Also, a \(2\)UBE of Type  for \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) is incompatible with more than one \(2\)UBE of Type  for its children, unless one of them is a single edge. If no child of \(\mu \) is such that the \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) is of Type , then the \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) would have Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: About the sufficiency: we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have: the Q-node \(\omega \), if any, with Type , followed by \(\xi \), if any, followed by the remaining children of \(\mu \) in any order; see Fig. 17c. \(\square \)
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: Conditions for Type . Let \(\mu \) be a P-node, we define two cases:
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: NBR.1 All children of \(\mu \) admit either Type  or Type , and there exist at least a child that admits Type  and a child that admits Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: NBR.2 There exists a child of \(\mu \) that admits Type  and all other children admit either Type  or Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: (Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if Case NBR.1 or NBR.2 holds.
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: It is easy to see that these conditions are necessary. In fact, for any child \(\mu _i\) of \(\mu \), the restriction of a \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  to \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu _i})\) is clearly of Type \(\langle X, B, R\rangle \), with \(X \in \{R,L,N\}\), since a \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of type \(\langle S, B, T\rangle \) implies that the embeddings of the \(2\)UBEs of the pertinent graphs of all children of \(\mu \) must be of type \(\langle S', B, T'\rangle \), with \(S,S',T,T' \in \{R,L,N\}\).
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: Fig. 18Constructions for P-nodes of Type Full size image
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: For the sufficiency, both in Case NBR.1 and in Case NBR.2 we order the children from left to right in such a way that children with \(2\)UBEs of Type  precede all children with \(2\)UBEs of Type ; see Figs.18a,18b. In Case NBR.2, the child with \(2\)UBE of Type  is placed between the last child with \(2\)UBE of Type , if any, and the first child with \(2\)UBE of Type , if any; see Fig. 18b. \(\square \)
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node, we define two cases:
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: RBN.1 All children of \(\mu \) admit either Type  or Type , and there exist at least a child that admits Type  and a child that admits Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: RBN.2 There exists a child of \(\mu \) that admits Type  and all other children admit either Type  or Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: (Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if Case RBN.1 or RBN.2 holds.
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: Cases RBN.1 and RBN.2 are symmetric to Cases NBR.1 and NBR.2, i.e., they can be obtained from Cases NBR.1 and NBR.2 after a vertical flip of the realizable embedding types of the children of \(\mu \). Thus, the necessity and sufficiency proofs are symmetric to those presented in Lemma 16. \(\square \)
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node. We define six cases:
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: NBN.1 There exists a child of \(\mu \) that admits Type  and the other children admit either Type  or Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: NBN.2 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into three sets as follows. The first set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type . The second set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type . The third set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: NBN.3 This case is obtained from Case NBN.2 by considering the types admitted by its children after a vertical flip.
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: NBN.4–NBN.6 These cases are obtained from Cases NBN.1–NBN.3, respectively, by considering the horizontally-mirrored embedding types for the children of \(\mu \).
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: (Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if at least one of Cases NBN.1–NBN.6 holds.
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: Since Type  is self-symmetric, we have that, for each case NBN.1–NBN.3, we can obtain a symmetric one (i.e., cases NBN.4–NBN.6) by reversing the left-to-right sequence of the children in the construction and by taking, for each child, the horizontally-mirrored embedding type. Thus, for each pair of symmetric cases, we only describe one of them.
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: For the sufficiency, we consider the three cases separately. In Case NBN.1 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the children that admit Type  (if any), in any order, followed by the unique child that admits Type , followed by the children that admit Type  (if any), in any order; see Fig. 19a. In Case NBN.2 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the possible children that admit Type  (if any), in any order, followed by the possible child that admits only Type  (if any), followed by the possible children that admit Type  (if any), in any order, followed by the possible child that admits only Type  (if any), followed by the possible children that admit Type  (if any), in any order (see Fig. 19b). In Case NBN.3 we proceed as in Case NBN.2 by considering the types after a vertical flip. Clearly, these orderings allow for a \(2\)UBE of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type .
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: The necessity can be argued by considering that in any 2UBE \(\gamma \) of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  the following hold: at most one of the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle N ,\cdot , \cdot \rangle \); at most one of the restriction of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle \cdot ,\cdot , N \rangle \); and all the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) are of Type \(\langle \cdot ,B, \cdot \rangle \). \(\square \)
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node. We define two cases:
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: RRN.1 The children of \(\mu \) can be partitioned into two sets: The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: RRN.2 The children of \(\mu \) can be partitioned into three sets: The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The second part consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type . The third set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: (Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if Case RRN.1 or Case RRN.2 holds.
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: Observe that Cases RRN.1 and RRN.2 are symmetric to Cases NRR.1 and NRR.2 (see Lemma 12), i.e., they can be obtained from Cases NRR.1 and NRR.2 after a vertical flip of the types of the children. The necessity and sufficiency proofs are symmetric to those presented in Lemma 12. \(\square \)
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node, we define six cases:
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: NRN.1 There exists a child that admits Type  and the other non-Q-node children admit Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: NRN.2 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into three sets as follows. The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type . The third set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: NRN.3 This case is obtained from Case NRN.2 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: NRN.4 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into two sets as follows. The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: NRN.5 It is possible to partition the children of \(\mu \) into three sets as follows. The first set consists either of a Q-node child or of a non-Q-node child that admits Type , or both. The second set consists of any number, even zero, of children that admit Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type . The third set consists of at least one child that admits Type  or Type , with at most one child that admits only Type .
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: NRN.6 This case is obtained from Case NRN.5 by considering the types of the children of \(\mu \) after a vertical flip.
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: (Type ) A P-node \(\mu \) admits Type  in the variable embedding setting if and only if one of Cases NRN.1–NRN.6 holds.

text_blocks.text: For the sufficiency, we consider the six cases separately. In Case NRN.1 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (if any), with Type , followed by the unique Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order; see Fig. 20a.
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: In Case NRN.2 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (if any), with Type , followed by the Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the unique Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order; see 20b. In Case NRN.3 we proceed as in Case NRN.2 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NRN.4 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (if any), with Type , followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the unique Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order; see Fig. 20c. In Case NRN.5 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (if any), with Type , followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the unique Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the unique Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order; see Fig. 20d. In Case NRN.6 we proceed as in Case NRN.5 by considering the types after a vertical flip.
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: The necessity can be argued by considering that in any 2UBE \(\gamma \) of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  we have the following: at most one of the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle N ,\cdot , \cdot \rangle \); at most one of the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle \cdot ,\cdot , N \rangle \); and that all the restrictions of \(\gamma \) on the children of \(\mu \) must be of Type \(\langle \cdot ,R, \cdot \rangle \) or of Type \(\langle \cdot ,B, \cdot \rangle \). However, not all the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) may be of Type \(\langle \cdot ,B, \cdot \rangle \) as otherwise the resulting embeddings would be of Type \(\langle \cdot ,B, \cdot \rangle \). \(\square \)

text_blocks.text: Conditions for Type. Let \(\mu \) be a P-node. In order to describe when \(\mu \) is of Type , we need to define twenty-six cases. We assume that Case NNN.i applies only if Cases NNN.k, with \(1 \le k \le i-1\), do not apply.

text_blocks.text: Since Type  is self-symmetric, we only need to consider the Cases NNN.1–NNN.13; the Cases NNN.14–NNN.26 being analogous. In Case NNN.1 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child, with Type , followed by the Type  child; see Fig. 21a. In Case NNN.2 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by Q-node child (if any), with Type ; see Fig 21b. In Case NNN.3 we proceed as follows. If there exists no Type  non-Q-node child, we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case), with Type , followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case). See Fig. 21c. Otherwise (there exists one Type  non-Q-node child), we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  non-Q-node child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the Type  Q-node child, if any. See Fig.21d. In Case NNN.4 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the Type  Q-node child (if any). See Fig. 22a. In Case NNN.5 we proceed as in Case NNN.4 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NNN.6 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  child, followed by any number of Type  children, followed by the child that admits only Type  (if any), followed by the Q-node child (if any), with Type ; see Fig. 22b. In Case NNN.7 we proceed as in Case NNN.6 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NNN.8 we proceed as follows. If there exists no Type  non-Q-node child, we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case), with Type , followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (if any) followed by the Type  children, if any, in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case). See Fig. 22c . Otherwise (there exists one Type  non-Q-node child), we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  non-Q-node child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (if any), followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the Q-node child (if any), with Type ; see Fig. 22d. In Case NNN.9 we proceed as in Case NNN.8 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NNN.10 we proceed as follows. If there exists no Type  non-Q-node child, we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Q-node child (which exists by the conditions of the case), with Type , followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (which exists by the conditions of the case). See Fig. 23a. Otherwise (there exists one Type  non-Q-node child), we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the Type  non-Q-node child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  child (if any), followed by the Q-node child (if any), with Type . See Fig. 23b. In Case NNN.11 we proceed as in Case NNN.10 by considering the types after a vertical flip. In Case NNN.12 we order the children of \(\mu \) so that from left to right we have the non-Q-node Type  child, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  children (if any), in any order, followed by the Type  non-Q-node child (if any), followed by the Q-node child (if any), with Type ; see Fig. 23c. In Case NNN.13 we proceed as in Case NNN.12 by considering the types after a vertical flip.
text_blocks.label: author_candidate

text_blocks.text: Fig. 23Constructions for P-nodes of Type . Subfigures (a) and (b) show the constructions for Case NNN.10 when there exists no Type  non-Q-node child and when there exists such a child, respectivelyFull size image
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: Constructions for P-nodes of Type . Subfigures (a) and (b) show the constructions for Case NNN.10 when there exists no Type  non-Q-node child and when there exists such a child, respectively
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: Constructions for P-nodes of Type . Subfigures (a) and (b) show the constructions for Case NNN.10 when there exists no Type  non-Q-node child and when there exists such a child, respectively
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: Constructions for P-nodes of Type . Subfigures (a) and (b) show the constructions for Case NNN.10 when there exists no Type  non-Q-node child and when there exists such a child, respectively
text_blocks.label: content

text_blocks.text: The necessity can be argued by considering that in any 2UBE \(\gamma \) of \({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\) of Type  we have the following: at most one of the restriction of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle N ,\cdot , \cdot \rangle \) and at most one of the restrictions of \(\gamma \) to the children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle \cdot ,\cdot , N \rangle \) (notice that these two children may coincide in a single child of Type \(\langle N ,\cdot , N \rangle \)); at most one of the restriction of \(\gamma \) to the non-Q-node children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle L, L, L \rangle \) and at most one of the restriction of \(\gamma \) to the non-Q-node children of \(\mu \) is of Type \(\langle R, R, R \rangle \). \(\square \)
